text: Cyber-physical systems (CPSs) form the core of today’s critical infrastructure, and a multitude of failures occurring on a daily basis pose a serious threat to the operational safety of these systems due to faults and anomalies. This work explores the applicability of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) — namely Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 (8B), and Gemma 2 — on the anomaly detection task in the battery management and powertrain domains. Furthermore, a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) based approach is proposed, utilizing a two-stage method that extracts the underlying operational rules based on the provided fault-free system data and then applies them for anomaly detection. The achieved experimental results confirm that the standard prompt works well on battery data, but only leads to limited success with the powertrain data. An adjusted version of the prompt — which is constructed by introducing a rule-focused keyword — ultimately enhances the performance of the models. The best F1-score values are 0.99 achieved with Mistral 7B, and 1.0 that are reached by Llama 3.1 and Gemma 2 for specific cases. Overall, our experimental results demonstrate that optimal prompting and structured rule extraction are crucial for the applicability of LLMs in enhancing the reliability of the CPSs.
